topics.topic_title: INTRODUCTION TO ART, CRAFTS AND DESIGN

sub_topics.title: ART AWARENESS AND APPRECIATION

teacher_steps: Encourage learners to define the meaning of art in general by displaying different pictures depicting various art branches (Performing Arts, Visual Arts, Fine Arts and Digital Arts)
Guide learners to categorize art into performing (like dance and theatre…), literary (poetry, stories…), and visual (paintings, sculptures…) art forms
learner_steps: Discuss what art means to them
Categorize art into performing, literary, and visual art forms
Create posters or digital collages illustrating each type of art, explaining their definitions

teacher_steps: Organize a gallery walk or virtual tour where examples of visual art, performing arts (videos of dance performances or theatre excerpts), and digital art (websites, digital illustrations) are exhibited
In an event of not having gallery facilities in a locality, prepare a selection of artworks, videos or digital images representing different branches of art
Use multi-media to enhance the learning process
learner_steps: Undertake a gallery walk or virtual tour in groups
Create a mind map or infographic categorizing different branches of art discovered, with examples and brief descriptions for each

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Visiting Different Places Where Art is Found
teacher_steps: Give learners a structured observation guide including: work titles/message interpretation and how the work resonates with the learners' insight
Co-ordinate with local art institutions or arrange guest speakers/artists, if the field trip is not possible
learner_steps: Visit local art galleries, museums, cultural centres or out-door murals (if physical visits are not feasible, take virtual tours of museums e.g google arts and culture sites)
Document their observations as per tour-study findings
Prepare a short oral presentation about their favourite piece of art from the visit, discussing why it resonates with them

teacher_steps: Teach basic art critique methods to learners (how to analyse, interpret, describe, judge …)
Encourage positive discussion where varied interpretations are given
Provide learners with different artworks (prints, digital images, or videos) representing various styles and periods
Guide them through structured critique sessions where art elements are conveyed and interpreted
Guide learners in the process of identifying and critiquing various artworks
learner_steps: Critique identified/readily made artefacts they were exposed to during art gallery reviews
Write a critique or create a video presentation sharing analysis of one artwork

sub_topics.short_notes: In this Subtopic, the focus is on art Awareness and Appreciation which covers categories of art which includes, Visual arts such as painting, drawing, sculpture, photography and print making. Learners should be guided to comprehend Applied Arts which focuses on practical skills. Learners should be introduced to various categories and branches of art, so that they develop a deeper understanding and valuing of the arts, enriching their lives and broader cultural experiences. Art awareness and appreciation covers categories of art which includes visual arts (painting, drawing, sculpture, photography and print making) performing arts (music, dance, theater and film) and literary arts (poetry, fiction, drama, and creative writing). The branches of Art include Fine Art, Applied Arts and Decorative Arts by applying appreciation skills such as observation, analysis, evaluation and contextualization. Learners should be exposed to Art movements and styles like Renaissance Art, Modern Art and Contemporary Art.

vocabulary.definition: crafts, painting, drawing, sculpture, photography
music, dance, theatre
poetry, fiction, drama
Art forms created primarily for aesthetic purposes
Objects made by humans, typically of cultural or historical interest

materials: On-line resources such as art station, YouTube
Ready-made photos/illustrations/pictures
Visual arts resources such as sketchbooks, paper, paint
Artifacts
Sound systems

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that learners develop a deeper comprehension and valuing of the arts. Prepare learning tasks during (learning sessions) and after (carry-home tasks) the allocated time for practical and oral assessments based on Branches of Art to determine competences of the learner.

sub_topics.title: CAREERS IN ART AND DESIGN

teacher_steps: Invite guest speakers from various art careers to share their insights and experiences
Engage in online tutorials related to art careers
learner_steps: List different careers related to Art and Design and categorize them under various fields (education, fashion, museum work, digital art, fine arts…)
Dramatize various art careers
Create an art work to do with an art career of their choice (the learner must exhibit job description, required skills…)

teacher_steps: Introduce famous artists from different time periods and styles (Henry Tayali, Cynthia Zukas, Caleb Chisha…)
Provide different paintings or sculptures and ask learners to analyze styles, techniques, and common themes used by artists
Provide clues or hints about artists' unique styles to help learners make connections
learner_steps: Discuss images of famous artworks and match them to their creators
Research on different artists and create short biographies and present their findings

sub_topics.short_notes: In this subtopic, learners shall be introduced to some job opportunities in Art so that they can understand the job descriptions of various areas of Art and Design such as, General Arts, Fine Arts, Crafts and Commercial Art. Under careers in Art and Design, there are a number of job descriptions which include: Art Teachers, Lecturers, Curators, Painters, Drawers, Sculptors, Craftsmen, Potters, Textile artists, Jewellers, Weavers, Sign Writers, Graphic Designers, Furniture Designer, Fashion Designer, Architects, Product Designers, Interior Designers, Illustrators, Cartoonist, Landscape Designers.

vocabulary.definition: Professional who teaches art in educational institutions
Professional responsible for overseeing and managing artefacts or other items of historical or cultural significance
Professional who creates visual content using design software
Professional who designs patterns and textiles

materials: Charts and diagrams on art careers
Videos/documentaries about professionals in different art fields
Printed career profiles with job descriptions and educational requirements
Art and Design textbooks and literature
Matching Artworks to Specific Artists

assessment.description: Learners should be tasked to mention/write what is involved in a number of listed art related careers. For example, an art curator: is a professional responsible for overseeing and managing artefacts or other items of historical or cultural significance.

sub_topics.title: VISUAL ARTS

teacher_steps: Encourage learners to conduct critical discussions when identifying art forms
learner_steps: Identify and classify images of different artworks into Fine Arts (painting, sculpture…), Crafts (pottery, weaving…) and Design (graphic design, fashion...)

instructional_blocks.method: Gallery Walk and Discussion, Comparative Analysis
teacher_steps: Set up an exhibition of the different art forms in the classroom
Arrange the displays into Fine Arts (painting, sculpture…), Crafts (pottery, weaving…) and Design (graphic design, fashion...) categories
Encourage learners to conduct critical discussions during the sorting and classifying processes
learner_steps: Examine different artworks, identifying materials, techniques and purposes of each form
Analyze their styles, cultural significance and artistic techniques

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Discussing the Functions of Visual Arts
teacher_steps: Provide real-life examples of artists who earn a living from their art (fashion designers, painters, sculptors)
Divide the class into groups to discuss the importance of Visual Arts in economic and cultural development
learner_steps: Identify local artists and analyze how their work contributes to society

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Producing Artworks Using Forms of Visual Arts
teacher_steps: Use real-world examples to make lessons relatable. Encourage learners to bring in items from home that represent Fine Arts, Crafts, or Design
Encourage creativity by allowing students to experiment with different materials and techniques
Use multimedia resources (videos, slides, online galleries) to expose learners to diverse art forms
Facilitate group discussions to help learners appreciate different perspectives on Visual Arts
Provide constructive feedback during practical sessions to motivate learners
learner_steps: Create simple artworks using different materials (painting, sculpture, textile art)
Produce crafts (beadwork, clay modeling, or fabric painting…)

sub_topics.short_notes: In this subtopic, learners shall be exposed to Visual Arts and the different categories (Fine Arts, Crafts, Design). This means communicating by creating art that can be seen. Therefore, Visual Art is a form of visual communication. Visual Arts covers categories of art which includes, Visual arts such as painting, drawing, sculpture, photography and print making. Learners should be guided to comprehend Applied Arts which focuses on practical skills.

vocabulary.definition: Art forms that create works which are primarily visual in nature
Art forms developed primarily for aesthetics rather than utility
Activities involving skill in making things by hand
The art or action of conceiving and planning the form and structure of something

materials: Printed images of various forms of Visual Arts
Art materials (paints, clay, fabric, paper, beads) for practical activities
Videos and documentaries showcasing artists and their work
Reference books and articles on Visual Arts
Local artefacts and crafts for analysis and discussion

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners correctly identify and analyze the different categories of Visual Arts. Prepare the tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments during and after the lesson, based on different forms of Visual Arts.
assessment.expected_standard: Forms of visual Arts identified correctly

sub_topics.title: PIONEERS OF ZAMBIAN VISUAL ARTS
sub_topics.specific_competence: Identify pioneers of Zambian Visual Arts

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Identifying Pioneers of Zambian Visual Arts
teacher_steps: Provide learners with images of artworks and short artist descriptions and have them match each artist to their work
Research work and pre-tasks to be given to learners prior to the lessons
learner_steps: Research and create short biographies, highlighting Zambian Visual Art Pioneers (Henry Tayali, Gabriel Ellison, Cynthia Zukas, Akwila Simpasa, Godfrey Setti, Shadreck Simukanga, and Martin Abasi Phiri…)

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Critiquing Works of Pioneer Visual Artists
teacher_steps: Provide context by discussing Zambia's Art History and how Visual Arts have developed over time
Encourage independent research by assigning artists to learners to investigate
Use multimedia resources such as videos, online galleries and books to provide visual examples of the artists' works
Promote critical thinking by guiding learners to analyze how historical and social factors influenced pioneer artists
Facilitate group discussions to compare different artistic styles and perspectives
learner_steps: Provide learners with images or descriptions of famous Zambian artworks and guide them through a structured critique, analyzing elements such as technique, theme, and cultural significance
Discuss how pioneer artists have influenced contemporary Zambian art
Prepare and present critiques of selected works, focusing on artistic style, message and historical impact

sub_topics.short_notes: This subtopic will discuss pioneers of the Zambian Visual Arts. Over the past 30 years there has been an increase in the number of Zambian artists. The term 'Zambian' used in this context, includes any artist working and living in Zambia. Some of the prominent artists in Zambia were trained at Evelyn Hone College which was opened just before independence and through its courses has trained a large number of the artists and designers, others furthered their studies in universities abroad. Some of the pioneering artists include: Henry Tayali, Gabriel Ellison, Cynthia Zukas, Akwila Simpasa, Godfrey Setti, Shadreck Simukanga, Martin Abasi Phiri. Leaners shall explore pioneers of Zambian Visual artists and their works.

vocabulary.definition: A person who is among the first to explore or settle a new area or field of activity
An artist who was among the first to develop visual arts in a particular region

materials: Images and prints of artworks by Zambian Visual Arts Pioneers
Books, articles, and online resources on Zambian Visual Arts Pioneers
Documentaries and interviews featuring Zambian Visual Arts Pioneers
Museum or gallery visits (physical or virtual)
Biographical notes on Henry Tayali, Gabriel Ellison, and other Visual Arts Pioneers
Readily-made art works
Various paintings
Online resources (museum, websites or art databases…)

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners identify pioneers of Zambian Visual artists (sculptors, painters, graphic designers, potters…) and their works. Prepare tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments based on pioneers of Zambian Visual Artists.
assessment.expected_standard: Pioneers of Zambian Visual Arts identified appropriately

sub_topics.title: ELEMENTS OF ART
sub_topics.specific_competence: Apply Art Elements in practical activities

teacher_steps: Guide learners to identify, discuss and analyse Art Elements
learner_steps: Identify, discuss and analyse Art Elements (Line, shape, space, value, texture, tone and colour)
Experiment with Art Elements to express and communicate ideas (Using lines to create contour, gesture drawings and calligraphy writing…)

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Analysing the use of shape, form and space
teacher_steps: Demonstrate before practice to help learners understand techniques
Use different art tools to make learning interactive and engaging
Start with basics: Introduce primary shapes (square, circle, triangle, rectangle) and explore their properties
Use real-life examples: Point out shapes in everyday objects, architecture, and nature
Explore positive and negative space: Discuss the relationship between objects and the space around them
learner_steps: Explore using basic geometric shapes to create abstract designs of shape, form and space

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Creating Artworks with the use of shape, form and space
teacher_steps: Demonstrate before practice to help learners understand techniques
Use different art tools to make learning interactive and engaging
learner_steps: Make a mixed media art project by combining shape, form and space in a single artwork

sub_topics.short_notes: In this topic the learners shall be exposed to the significance of Elements of Art. This results in making of illustrations (fabric, advertisement, packaging, books, stationery...) It is therefore important that, Art elements are known to be parts used to make artwork (line, shape, tone, form colour, texture and pattern...).

vocabulary.definition: A mark made by a moving point
A two-dimensional area with defined boundaries
A three-dimensional object with volume
The area around, between, and within objects
The surface quality of an object
The lightness or darkness of a color
The visual perception of different wavelengths of light

materials: Ready-made art works
Online resources such as websites or art databases
Drawing paper, sketchbooks, coloured pencils, paints, and brushes
Calligraphy pens or markers

assessment.description: Provide constructive feedback: Offer feedback that encourages learners to refine their understanding of elements of Art (Line, shape, space, value, texture, tone and colour). Prepare the tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments based on elements of art.
assessment.expected_standard: Art elements in practical activities applied correctly

sub_topics.title: PRINCIPLES OF ART
sub_topics.specific_competence: Create an Art work showing prominent use of one or more of the Art principles

teacher_steps: Guide learners to define the Art Principles (balance, proportion, emphasis, movement, pattern, unity, contrast…) and provide examples of each principle in various artworks
learner_steps: Collect readily made images (illustrations, photographs, magazine image cut-outs…)

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Discussing the use of Art Principles as applied in Art works
teacher_steps: Provide examples of artworks that effectively use one or more art principles
Use visual aids such as diagrams, charts, and examples of artworks to help learners understand and remember the art principles
Encourage learners to experiment and explore when creating their artworks, as this will help them develop their creativity and problem-solving skills
Encourage learners to interact and engage critically during the analytical stages
learner_steps: Analyze and discuss how artists use Art Principles to create composition and the meaning in their artworks
Create artworks that demonstrate art principles
Experiment with different materials and techniques to create unique artworks
Discuss and display their artworks

sub_topics.short_notes: This sub-topic will discuss Principles of Art which serve as the foundation for creating visually appealing and effective artworks. By understanding and applying these principles, learners will be able to exhibit their creativity and produce innovative artworks that showcase their unique perspective (balance, proportion, rhythm, harmony and space…)

vocabulary.definition: The distribution of visual weight in an artwork
The size relationship of parts to the whole and to each other
The focal point or area of greatest interest in an artwork
The path the viewer's eye takes through the artwork
The repetition of elements in a predictable manner
The feeling that all parts of the artwork belong together
The difference between elements in an artwork

materials: Art textbooks or online resources that provide examples and explanations of the Art Principles
Art materials and tools (paints, markers, coloured pencils, paper…) that learners can use to create their artworks

assessment.description: You should make an assessment to ensure that the learners adequately apply the art principles in practical activities. Prepare the tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments based on art principles.
assessment.expected_standard: Artwork showing prominent use of one or more of the Art principles created appropriately

sub_topics.title: BASIC SHAPES

teacher_steps: Show pictures or objects of the basic shapes to learners and ask them to identify each shape
learner_steps: Define and explain the basic shapes (cuboid, pyramid, cone, rectangular box…)

teacher_steps: Use real-life objects to demonstrate the basic shapes and make it more relatable for learners
Encourage learners to experiment with different shapes and combinations to develop their critical thinking and creativity
learner_steps: Draw simple objects using basic shapes, such as a house (rectangular box), a tree (cylinder), or a car (cuboid)
Use basic shapes to create their own creative drawings, such as animal, a robot, or a fantasy creature

sub_topics.short_notes: This sub-topic will discuss Principles of Art which serve as the foundation for creating visually appealing and effective artworks. By understanding and applying these principles, learners will be able to exhibit their creativity and produce innovative artworks that showcase their unique perspective (balance, proportion, rhythm, harmony and space…). Basic shapes form the foundation of drawing and include cuboid, pyramid, cone, rectangular box, and other geometric forms.

vocabulary.definition: A three-dimensional rectangular shape
A three-dimensional shape with a polygonal base and triangular sides meeting at a point
A three-dimensional shape with a circular base tapering to a point
A three-dimensional shape with two parallel circular bases

materials: Geometric shape blocks or puzzles to help learners visualize and identify the basic shapes
Drawing paper and pencils or markers for learners to practice drawing and applying basic shapes

assessment.description: Observation of learners during drawing activities. Review of learners' drawings for accuracy and creativity. Quizzes or tests to assess learners' understanding of basic shapes.
assessment.expected_standard: Basic shapes applied in drawings correctly

sub_topics.title: TYPES OF SHADING

teacher_steps: Demonstrate each technique on the board
Explain that hatching and cross-hatching create texture and contrast
Explain that pointillism is best for subtle shading effects
Explain that smudging gives smooth transitions between light and dark areas
Explain that scribbling can create rough textures
learner_steps: Observe and discuss different shading techniques (pointillism, hatching, cross-hatching, smudging, scribbling, smashing)
Analyze real-life objects or famous drawings to identify shading techniques used by artists

teacher_steps: Explain that the direction of the light source affects shading
Explain that darker tones represent shadows, while lighter tones represent highlights
Explain that gradual transitions make drawings look more realistic
learner_steps: Create a simple drawing of a GREY scale from WHITE to BLACK using at least any shading techniques of their choice
Each learner will explain their choice of shading techniques and the effects achieved

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Applying Various Shading Techniques in a Composition
teacher_steps: Apply light source principles to the shading exercises
Explain that different shading techniques can be combined for texture and contrast
Explain that smudging can create a smooth transition, while hatching can define sharp edges
Explain that layering cross-hatching increases depth and darkness
learner_steps: Practice tonal gradation by shading a sphere or cube from dark to light using different shading techniques

sub_topics.short_notes: Shading techniques help artists create realistic drawings by showing the effect of light and shadow. Different techniques allow for different textures and moods in artwork. This subtopic will explore various shading techniques and their application in drawings. Shading enhances realism in drawings by adding depth, light, and shadow. There are different shading techniques: Pointillism (stippling) uses dots for shading, Hatching uses parallel lines, Cross-hatching uses overlapping lines for darker tones, Smudging involves blending for smooth transitions, Scribbling uses freehand scribbles for texture, and Mashing involves pressing and smudging for rough textures. Tonal gradation transitions from dark to light. Effective shading depends on light source and material choice.

vocabulary.definition: A shading technique using dots
A shading technique using parallel lines
A shading technique using overlapping lines
A shading technique involving blending for smooth transitions
A shading technique using freehand scribbles
The transition from dark to light tones

materials: Samples of shaded drawings
Printed or digital images of artworks with shading
Graphite pencils, charcoal, and erasers
Visual aids (posters or slides)
Objects (cubes, spheres…) for light and shadow observation
Graphite pencils (HB, 2B, 4B, 6B…)
Blending tools (tissue paper, blending stumps…)

assessment.description: Observation: Monitor learners' shading application during practical work. Practical Work: Learners produce a shaded artwork using different techniques. Oral Questioning: Ask learners to explain how and why they used specific shading techniques.
assessment.expected_standard: Apply various shading techniques appropriately to create tonal variations in drawings

sub_topics.title: DIFFERENT TEXTURES
sub_topics.specific_competence: Compose pictures using a variety of textures and values

teacher_steps: Show learners' examples of artworks that demonstrate different textures and values, ask them to identify and describe the textures and values used
learner_steps: Define and explain the art elements of texture and value and provide examples of each

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Identifying Different Textures (Actual and Visual Texture)
teacher_steps: Provide learners with various materials that demonstrate actual texture, such as sandpaper, velvet, ask them to identify and describe the textures
Use a variety of materials and examples to demonstrate different textures and values
Encourage learners to experiment and explore further when creating their artworks
learner_steps: Identify and describe different visual textures
Create artworks that demonstrate a variety of textures, values and shading using different materials and techniques
Experiment with different textures and values to create unique and interesting effects

sub_topics.short_notes: This subtopic exposes the learners to different textures. Texture can be split into various categories (actual, visual, natural, man-made…). These are just a few examples of many different textures that exist. Texture can add depth, interest and emotion to an artwork. The focus in this study will be on texture and value.

vocabulary.definition: The surface quality or feel of an object
The physical texture you can feel
The illusion of texture created visually
The lightness or darkness of a color or tone

materials: Various materials that demonstrate actual texture, such as sandpaper and velvet
Art supplies, such as paints, markers, and coloured pencils, for learners to create their textures

assessment.description: Observation of learners during art-making activities. Review of learners' artworks for texture, value, and shading.
assessment.expected_standard: Pictures composed using a variety of textures and values appropriately

sub_topics.title: PRE-HISTORIC ROCK ART OF ZAMBIA
sub_topics.specific_competence: Analyse the Pre-Historic Rock Art of Zambia

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Analysing the Pre-historic rock Art of Zambia
teacher_steps: Prepare interactive activities
Define Rock art, and its significance
Display images or videos of different art styles, and techniques
Describe the materials and techniques used to create Rock-art
Discuss the cultural significance, spiritual, historical importance of rock art
learner_steps: Identify the Pre-historic rock Art sites of Zambia

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Researching on the pre-historic arts of Zambia
teacher_steps: Guide research on materials and tools, themes, styles on Pre-historic arts of Zambian painting
learner_steps: Research on materials and tools, themes, styles on Pre-historic arts of Zambian painting

sub_topics.short_notes: In this topic, explain to the learners the importance of prehistoric rock art as it will help to promote its preservation and promotion. Zambia is home to a rich collection of pre-historic rock art. The Rock paintings depict a range of themes including human figures, animals and abstract designs. Additionally, it has cultural importance as it provides valuable insights into beliefs, practices and cultural patterns of people who created the paintings. Zambia has a number of other rock art sites, which feature geometric and figurative rock art traditions. These rock art sites are not only important cultural and historical treasures but also provide a unique window into the lives and experiences of Zambia's ancient inhabitants. Pre-Historic Rock Art is referred as to the images, symbols and designs created by ancient humans on rocks, caves, wall and other stone surfaces during the Pre-historic period, which spans from the emergence of early humans to the development of written records.

vocabulary.definition: The period before written records
Images, symbols and designs created on rock surfaces
Documents or inscriptions that record information
Belonging to the very distant past

materials: Online resources such as websites or art databases
Reference books
Videos

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners analyse Pre-Historic Rock Art of Zambia. Prepare the tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments based on local Pre-Historic Rock Art of Zambia.
assessment.expected_standard: Pre-historic Rock Art of Zambia analysed correctly

sub_topics.title: TRADITIONAL CRAFTS

teacher_steps: Encourage learners to freely explore and experiment with various working media
Take safety pre-cautionary measures and observations on social, cultural and environmental norms/accessories/working tools
learner_steps: Make various traditional crafts (reed mats, stools, mortar and pestle...)

sub_topics.short_notes: Zambia has a rich cultural heritage and traditional crafts play a significant role in preserving and showcasing this heritage. Some examples of traditional crafts include: Woodcarvings (Makishi masks, wooden figurines, functional and decorative wooden items), Basketry (woven basket, winnowing baskets and ornamental baskets), Textiles (chitenge, batik, embroidery), Ceramics (hand pottery, wheel thrown pottery), Metal work (blacksmithing), Other crafts (leatherwork, stone carvings, weaving). These traditional crafts not only showcase Zambia's rich cultural heritage but also provides a source of income for many local artisans.

vocabulary.definition: Skills and techniques passed down through generations for creating handmade objects
The art of shaping wood into decorative or functional objects
The craft of weaving baskets from natural materials
Traditional ceremonial masks from Zambia

materials: Online resources such as websites or art databases
Reference books
Videos

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners identified and analysed Pre-Historic Rock Art of Zambia. Prepare the tasks and allocate time during the material collection process and also practical/oral assessments based on Pre-Historic Rock Art of Zambia.

sub_topics.title: TYPES OF COLOURS

teacher_steps: Introduce the concept of the visible spectrum and its colours
Ask learners to draw and label the colors they observed
Have them write down their observations and any questions they may have
learner_steps: Identify the colours and their order

sub_topics.short_notes: Colour is what is perceived when waves of light strike the retina of the eye. The colour we see on objects is derived from artificial or natural light. Colour plays a vital role in various real-life situations, influencing our emotions, perceptions, and behaviours. Colour plays a vital role in various aspects of our lives, from marketing and art to safety and therapy. Understanding the importance of colour can help us make informed decisions, communicate more effectively, and appreciate the world around us. Colour is a powerful Element of Art that evokes emotion, conveys meaning and adds depth and interest to creations. In this subtopic, the world of colour relationships will be explored, learning about the colour wheel, primary and secondary colours, warm and cool colours and how to mix and match colours to create harmonious and contrasting effects.

vocabulary.definition: The relationship between or among groups of colours
What is perceived when waves of light strike retina of the eye
The name of a colour in its purest form
A colour where black has been added
A colour where white has been added
Matter that gives colour to materials such as paints, dyes, crayons, or inks

materials: Colour wheel diagrams
Colour spectrum poster
Colour theory handouts
Colour workbooks
Water colours, crayons…

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners experiment with colours of the spectrum, appropriately. Prepare tasks and allocate time for practical experimenting colours of the spectrum on a colour-wheel.
assessment.expected_standard: Experimenting colours of the spectrum (primary, secondary and tertiary)

sub_topics.title: COLOUR SCHEMES
sub_topics.specific_competence: Use colour schemes in painting exercises

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Using colour schemes in the painting exercises
teacher_steps: Provide learners with the appropriate colours
A sample of the colour chart/wheel should be provided
learner_steps: Create painted images/illustrations that represents a specific colour scheme and atmosphere

sub_topics.short_notes: These are combinations of colours used to create some beautiful sceneries. There are several types of colour schemes. When white light passes through a triangular-shaped piece of clear glass called a prism it is separated into the colours of the spectrum. Here are the main types of colour schemes: Monochromatic, Complementary, Analogous, Triadic, Warm and Cool.

vocabulary.definition: A colour scheme using variations of a single colour
A colour scheme using colours opposite each other on the colour wheel
A colour scheme using colours adjacent to each other on the colour wheel
A colour scheme using three colours equally spaced on the colour wheel

materials: Colour wheel diagrams
Colour schemes example: visual aids showing different colour schemes
Colour scheme work sheets
Colour theory workbooks
Online colour tutorials

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners used colour schemes in a painting activity, appropriately. Prepare tasks and allocate time for practical activities on colour schemes. Observe learners during the activity and assess their participation. Review their paintings. Conduct class discussions to assess learner skill acquisition.
assessment.expected_standard: Colour schemes used in the painting exercises appropriately

sub_topics.title: TOOLS AND MATERIALS
sub_topics.specific_competence: Use a variety of tools and materials for drawing and painting

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Discussing the use of various tools and materials for drawing
teacher_steps: Engage learners in a discussion on the characteristics and uses of each tool and material
learner_steps: Identify various working tools used for drawing (charcoal, chalk, pastels, crayons, pencils, pens…) and materials (paper, ink, walls, floors, fabrics…) through visual presentations and physical samples

teacher_steps: Divide Learners into groups to try out different tools on various materials
Explain that different papers absorb ink differently; crayons work best on textured surfaces; walls can be used for murals
learner_steps: Try out different tools on various materials
Each group presents their findings, discussing the effects of different tools on different surfaces

sub_topics.short_notes: This topic introduces learners to the fundamental tools and materials used in drawing and painting. It explores different tools such as charcoal, chalk, pastels, crayons, pencils and pens, as well as various materials like paper, ink, walls, floors, and fabrics. The importance of selecting and using appropriate tools and materials for artistic expression will be highlighted. Understanding the different tools and materials in art is essential for creating effective drawings and paintings. Each tool and material has unique properties that affect the final artwork. Studio practice in Art and Design refers to the practical activities that artists engage in within their studio environment. It encompasses a wide range of activities such as creating art, experimenting with different materials and techniques, conducting research, and exploring conceptual ideas.

vocabulary.definition: Implements used to create art (Charcoal, chalk, pastels, crayons, pencils, pens…)
Substances on which art is created (Paper, ink, walls, floors, fabrics)
The substance used in creating artwork
The feel or appearance of a surface
The arrangement of visual elements in an artwork

materials: Samples of tools and materials, pictures, demonstrational videos
Drawing papers, charcoal, pastels, crayons, pencils, ink, various surfaces, paper, fabric, walls

assessment.description: Observation: Checking how learners handle and use materials. Practical Work: Learners create an artwork using different tools and materials.
assessment.expected_standard: Variety of tools and materials for drawing used accordingly

sub_topics.title: TYPES OF DRAWING AND PAINTING
sub_topics.specific_competence: Identify types of drawing and painting

teacher_steps: Explain that realistic art closely resembles real objects, abstract art distorts reality, and non-objective art does not represent specific objects
Guide learners through the process of sketching, shading, and colouring
Encourage experimentation with different media and techniques to explore artistic expression
learner_steps: Present examples of realistic, abstract, and non-objective art
Analyze and categorize images based on their characteristics
Create their own artworks using one of the three styles

sub_topics.short_notes: Artists use different types of drawing and painting to express their creativity. The three main types under discussion include realistic, abstract, and non-objective art, each serving a unique purpose in communication and artistic representation.

vocabulary.definition: Art that closely resembles real objects
Art that distorts or simplifies reality
Art that does not represent specific objects
Methods used to create depth and dimension

materials: Drawing papers, paints, pencils, brushes, pastels

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners used the types of drawing/painting identified and applied correctly. Prepare the tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments based on types of drawing and painting.
assessment.expected_standard: Learners should correctly identify and apply different types of drawing and painting

sub_topics.title: FORMS OF DRAWING

teacher_steps: Introduce sketches, studies, and complete works through visual examples
Explain that sketches are quick and rough; studies are more detailed and focused on specific aspects; complete works are finished pieces
Emphasize the importance of planning in art, how sketches guide studies and how studies inform complete works
learner_steps: Observe and discuss the characteristics and purpose of each form
Practice making sketches of simple objects
Refine their sketches into detailed studies focusing on shading and texture
Create a complete drawing using elements from their sketches and studies

sub_topics.short_notes: Drawing takes various forms, each serving a different purpose. The three main forms are sketches, studies, and complete works, which artists use at different stages of their creative process.

vocabulary.definition: A quick, rough drawing
A detailed drawing focused on specific aspects
A finished artwork

materials: Samples of sketches, studies, and complete works, visual aids, art books
Drawing papers, pencils, charcoal, erasers, reference images

assessment.description: Make an assessment to ensure that the learners used the various Forms of Drawing, appropriately. Prepare the tasks and allocate time for practical and oral assessments based on Forms of Drawing.

sub_topics.title: LETTERING

teacher_steps: Teacher to paste standardised names to the written word tags
In the absence of computers, readily made standardised word tags shall be presented
learner_steps: Write their own names in any font (upper case, lower case, cursive, Calligraphy…)
Present various written names (word tags) and paste them on the wall, board or floor

teacher_steps: Provide readily made teaching aids on various styles of Calligraphy
Participate in a guided class discussion held to explore how calligraphy is different from other typefaces
learner_steps: Define and discuss a variety of Calligraphy styles and comment on the aesthetic appeals

teacher_steps: Arrange a variety of pre-arranged text materials (books, newspapers, posters, journals and other literature…) to appreciate the functions of lettering in case of lack of a library
Organize a local study tour to a nearby area that has a variety of signage so that learners can be exposed to different typefaces (billboards, sign posts, shop writings…)
learner_steps: Visit a library to look at different (books, newspapers, posters, journals…) literature to appreciate the functions of lettering

instructional_blocks.activity_title: Identifying careers relating to lettering works
teacher_steps: Collect various materials relating to lettering works and engage/expose learners in discussion sessions to match the appropriate careers aligned to the named products (Calligrapher-certificate writing…)
learner_steps: Match careers (sign writers, graphics designers, calligrapher, illustrator, Stationary designer…) relating to lettering works (packaging, posters, cards, billboards, magazines, newspapers, certificates…)

teacher_steps: Learners shall be exposed to standard lettering from A - Z
learner_steps: Design letters aesthetically in lower and upper cases in order to explore/experiment by manipulating the designed standardised lettering (Grid Lettering)

sub_topics.short_notes: The topic will cover Lettering, Poster making and Computer Aided Design (CAD). Graphic design helps learners develop their creative, technical and critical thinking skills; it also enhances opportunities by preparing them for the creative industry in careers to do with advertising, marketing, publishing and digital media. Lettering involves creation of standard (decorative or expressive) letterforms that can be used in Graphic Designs, Typography and Calligraphy. Most visual commercial communications produced incorporate text (lettering) and images (pictures). Modern trends utilize computers as a design tool to produce fascinating designs for different purposes/presentations.

vocabulary.definition: Large printed notices for display
Decorative handwriting or lettering
The art of arranging type
The arrangement of elements in a design
Design created using computer software
Small letters
Capital letters
The space between letters
The space between words
A small line attached to the end of a stroke in a letter

materials: Drawing boards
Plain papers
Compass
Eraser
Calligraphy pens
Pencils
Pens
Rulers

assessment.description: Observe learners as they practice different lettering techniques, such as hand-lettering, calligraphy, or typography. Evaluate a comprehensive lettering project, such as a poster, brochure or signage design, that demonstrate learners understanding of lettering principles and techniques.
